evaluation_inputs.system_description: The SNAM (Sistema Nacional de Alarma de Maremotos) is the Chilean national tsunami warning system. Its core function is to allow the generation and monitoring of tsunami warnings. The system captures seismic data, evaluates scenarios, and disseminates bulletins. A key component is the Warning Decision-making Subsystem, which interfaces with external entities like the National Seismic Center (CSN) and the National Emergency Office (ONEMI).

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The initial architectural design was provided as three specific views: Contextual, Functional, and Deployment. These were consistent with each other and previously revised by the Architect role.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: I have a question about the architecture.
I think that according to the difficulty and relevance of this attribute, it should be a priority of [value] due to [reason].

hardware_view_description: Deployment view includes an Information Capture Server, Decision Making Server, and Data Base Server.
Client terminals include ResearchClient, SensorsReader, ExpertClient, and BulletinClient.
External entities include CSN and ONEMI terminals.
runtime_constraints: Must operate in scenarios with No Internet connection or No phone connection.
Must prevent unauthorized external bulletin generation.

questions: How does the system handle alternative sending channels if the primary connection fails?
How are pre-modeled scenarios accessed if the primary database server fails?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Failure of the primary server data pre-modeled scenarios.
Attempt to generate newsletter externally by unauthorized users.

business_goals: Reduce maximum sending time of technical data from 1'20" to 50"
Reduce unofficial alert dissemination from 5% to 1%
Ensure unit goals are represented in the development and future operation
Generate and monitor tsunami warnings effectively
technical_constraints: System must integrate with external entities (CSN, ONEMI)
Must support alternative communication channels (e.g., if internet/phone fails)

children.scenario_description: Data transmission from CSN to SNAM completes in <50 seconds under normal seismic event conditions, including mandatory data validation steps (10-15s validation budget within 50s total)
SNAM to ONEMI bulletin dissemination completes in <2 minutes 30 seconds
Database query response time <5 seconds under normal operational load
System handles concurrent seismic events (multiple earthquakes in different regions) without performance degradation in warning dissemination
System maintains 99% uptime during seismic events
Primary server failure detected within 5 seconds of occurrence
Automatic service restoration to backup server within 10 seconds of primary failure detection
Communication redundancy ensures bulletin delivery despite network cuts or single-point failures (mapped from RS-01, RS-03)
Data integrity validation completes within allocated time budget (10-15s) without blocking warning dissemination, ensuring accuracy without compromising speed
Unauthorized bulletin generation attempts are blocked 100% of the time (mapped from RS-05)
System withstands DDoS attacks while maintaining core warning dissemination functions (mapped from RS-04)
Reduce unofficial alert dissemination from 5% to <1% through authentication mechanisms
Automated test coverage detects 90% of errors (improvement from current 25%)
System modifications deployable without exceeding 15 minutes of downtime using zero-downtime deployment architecture
Operational staff can diagnose and resolve system issues within 10 minutes using monitoring dashboards

scenarios.scenario_text: Data transmission from CSN to SNAM completes in less than 50 seconds under normal seismic event conditions, including mandatory data validation steps (10-15 second validation budget within the 50-second total window)
SNAM to ONEMI bulletin dissemination completes in less than 2 minutes 30 seconds along the critical warning path
Database query response time remains under 5 seconds under normal operational load for historical data access
System handles concurrent seismic events (multiple earthquakes in different geographic regions) without performance degradation in warning dissemination speed or accuracy
System maintains 99% uptime during seismic events, ensuring continuous monitoring and warning capabilities
Primary server failure is detected within 5 seconds of occurrence through health monitoring mechanisms
Automatic service restoration to backup server completes within 10 seconds of primary failure detection, minimizing warning dissemination interruption
Communication redundancy ensures bulletin delivery despite network cuts or single-point communication failures between CSN, SNAM, and ONEMI
Data integrity validation completes within allocated time budget (10-15 seconds) without blocking warning dissemination, ensuring accuracy without compromising life-safety response speed
Unauthorized bulletin generation attempts by external users are blocked 100% of the time through authentication and authorization controls
System withstands distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks while maintaining core warning dissemination functions through traffic filtering and priority queuing
Unofficial alert dissemination is reduced from 5% to less than 1% through multi-factor authentication mechanisms and role-based access control
Automated test coverage detects 90% of errors before deployment, representing significant improvement from the current 25% detection rate
System modifications are deployable without exceeding 15 minutes of downtime using zero-downtime deployment architecture (blue-green or rolling updates)
Operational staff can diagnose and resolve system issues within 10 minutes using centralized monitoring dashboards and diagnostic tools

scenario_reference.text: Data transmission from CSN to SNAM completes in <50 seconds under normal seismic event conditions, including mandatory data validation steps (10-15s validation budget within 50s total)

analysis_records.environment: Normal seismic event conditions with active data validation
analysis_records.stimulus: Seismic event detected by CSN sensors triggering data transmission to SNAM
architectural_decisions: Inline Data Validation Pipeline with parallel processing
Redundant Communication Paths for optimized routing
Direct network connections between CSN and SNAM
analysis_records.reasoning: The 50-second constraint requires validation to occur in parallel with transmission rather than sequentially. The inline validation pipeline processes data streams concurrently, allocating 10-15 seconds for validation within the total budget. Redundant paths enable route optimization and load balancing to minimize network latency.

risks.description: Validation pipeline complexity may introduce processing delays exceeding 15-second budget under high data volumes
risks.rationale: Complex validation algorithms combined with large seismic datasets could cause validation bottlenecks that violate the time constraint

risks.description: Network congestion on all redundant paths simultaneously could exceed 50-second total latency
risks.rationale: If multiple communication paths experience degradation concurrently, no fallback route exists to maintain performance

non_risks.description: Single path network failure will not violate latency requirement
non_risks.rationale: Redundant communication paths provide automatic failover with minimal switching overhead

scenario_reference.text: SNAM to ONEMI bulletin dissemination completes in <2 minutes 30 seconds

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with bulletin generation and dissemination active
analysis_records.stimulus: SNAM generates bulletin for public warning dissemination to ONEMI
architectural_decisions: Redundant Communication Paths between SNAM and ONEMI
Priority queuing for critical warning messages
Pre-established persistent connections
analysis_records.reasoning: The 150-second window is less critical than P1 but still requires reliable, fast delivery. Redundant paths ensure delivery despite single-point failures. Priority queuing (from DDoS mitigation layer) ensures warning messages bypass normal traffic. Persistent connections eliminate connection establishment overhead.

risks.description: ONEMI endpoint processing delays could consume significant portion of 150-second budget
risks.rationale: If ONEMI's receiving infrastructure has slow ingestion or acknowledgment mechanisms, end-to-end latency may be violated despite fast network transmission

non_risks.description: Network transmission time is unlikely to exceed budget given 150-second window
non_risks.rationale: Even with moderate network congestion, bulletin message sizes are small enough for transmission well under 150 seconds

conflicting_attributes: Performance (negative: larger message size, longer transmission)

scenario_reference.text: System handles concurrent seismic events (multiple earthquakes in different regions) without performance degradation in warning dissemination

analysis_records.environment: Multiple simultaneous seismic events in different geographic regions
analysis_records.stimulus: Concurrent earthquake detection in multiple regions triggering parallel warning workflows
analysis_records.response_measure: No degradation in P1 or P2 latency requirements under concurrent load
architectural_decisions: Parallel processing architecture with independent event pipelines
Resource partitioning for concurrent event handling
Load balancing across redundant communication paths
analysis_records.reasoning: Each seismic event must be processed independently without resource contention. Parallel pipelines ensure one event does not block another. Resource partitioning (CPU, memory, network bandwidth) prevents starvation. Load balancing distributes traffic across redundant paths to avoid bottlenecks.

risks.description: Resource exhaustion under extreme concurrent event scenarios (>5 simultaneous events) may cause performance degradation
risks.rationale: System capacity limits for CPU, memory, and network bandwidth may be exceeded if too many events occur simultaneously

risks.description: Validation pipeline may become bottleneck under concurrent load if not properly parallelized
risks.rationale: If validation resources are shared across events rather than partitioned, concurrent events will compete for validation capacity

non_risks.description: Two concurrent events will not cause performance degradation
non_risks.rationale: System is designed with sufficient resource headroom to handle typical concurrent event scenarios (2-3 events)

scenario_reference.text: System maintains 99% uptime during seismic events

analysis_records.environment: Seismic event conditions with potential infrastructure stress
analysis_records.stimulus: Seismic event occurs, potentially affecting system infrastructure
architectural_decisions: Hot-Standby Failover Architecture with active-passive configuration
Redundant Communication Paths for network resilience
Geographic distribution of critical components
analysis_records.reasoning: 99% uptime allows only ~87 hours downtime per year. Hot-standby failover ensures rapid recovery from primary failures. Redundant paths eliminate network single points of failure. Geographic distribution protects against localized infrastructure damage from seismic events.

risks.description: Correlated failures during major seismic events could affect both primary and standby systems
risks.rationale: If primary and standby systems are geographically co-located or share infrastructure, a major earthquake could disable both simultaneously

risks.description: State synchronization lag between primary and standby may cause data loss during failover
risks.rationale: If synchronization is not real-time, recent data may be lost when failing over to standby system

non_risks.description: Single component failure will not violate 99% uptime requirement
non_risks.rationale: Hot-standby architecture provides rapid failover (R2b: <10 seconds) which is negligible compared to annual uptime budget

sensitivity_points.parameter: Geographic separation distance between primary and standby

conflicting_attributes: Reliability (positive: less data loss on failover)

scenario_reference.text: Automatic service restoration to backup server within 10 seconds of primary failure detection

analysis_records.environment: Primary server failure detected by health monitoring
analysis_records.stimulus: Primary server failure detection triggers automatic failover
analysis_records.response_measure: Service restoration on backup server <10 seconds after detection
architectural_decisions: Hot-Standby Failover Architecture with pre-warmed backup
Automated failover orchestration without manual intervention
Continuous state synchronization to minimize recovery time
analysis_records.reasoning: 10-second recovery window requires backup server to be fully operational (hot standby) rather than cold standby. Automated orchestration eliminates human reaction time. Continuous state sync ensures backup has current data and can immediately assume primary role.

risks.description: Failover orchestration logic bugs could delay or prevent automatic recovery
risks.rationale: Complex failover logic may contain edge cases that cause orchestration failures, requiring manual intervention

risks.description: Network partition scenarios may cause split-brain condition with both primary and backup active
risks.rationale: If health monitoring incorrectly detects primary as failed due to network issues, both systems may attempt to serve requests simultaneously

non_risks.description: Backup server capacity is sufficient to handle full production load
non_risks.rationale: Hot-standby architecture requires backup to be identically provisioned to primary, ensuring no performance degradation post-failover

scenario_reference.text: Communication redundancy ensures bulletin delivery despite network cuts or single-point failures

analysis_records.environment: Network infrastructure degradation or partial failure
analysis_records.stimulus: Network path failure between critical system components
analysis_records.response_measure: Bulletin delivery continues via alternate paths without manual intervention
architectural_decisions: Redundant Communication Paths with diverse routing
Automatic path failover with health monitoring
Multiple independent network providers/technologies
analysis_records.reasoning: Single-point network failures must not disrupt critical warning dissemination. Diverse routing ensures paths do not share common failure points. Automatic failover eliminates manual switching delays. Multiple providers/technologies (e.g., fiber + satellite) protect against provider-specific outages.

risks.description: Shared infrastructure between 'redundant' paths may create hidden single points of failure
risks.rationale: If redundant paths share physical conduits, routers, or data centers, a single infrastructure failure could disable multiple paths

risks.description: Path failover logic may not detect degraded (but not failed) paths, causing performance issues
risks.rationale: If failover only triggers on complete path failure, severely degraded paths may remain in use, violating latency requirements

non_risks.description: Single network path failure will not prevent bulletin delivery
non_risks.rationale: Multiple independent paths ensure at least one route remains available for message delivery

conflicting_attributes: Cost (negative: higher infrastructure and maintenance costs)

scenario_reference.text: Data integrity validation completes within allocated time budget (10-15s) without blocking warning dissemination, ensuring accuracy without compromising speed

analysis_records.environment: Normal seismic event processing with concurrent validation
analysis_records.stimulus: Seismic data received requiring validation before bulletin generation
analysis_records.response_measure: Validation completes in 10-15 seconds without blocking critical path
architectural_decisions: Inline Data Validation Pipeline with parallel processing
Streaming validation architecture
Tiered validation with critical checks prioritized
analysis_records.reasoning: Validation must ensure data accuracy (reliability) while meeting strict timing constraints (performance). Inline pipeline processes data as it arrives rather than waiting for complete dataset. Streaming architecture validates chunks in parallel. Tiered approach performs critical checks first, deferring non-critical validation.

risks.description: Validation pipeline may pass corrupted data if validation algorithms have insufficient coverage
risks.rationale: Time-constrained validation may not detect all data corruption scenarios, allowing bad data to propagate to bulletins

risks.description: Parallel validation processes may have race conditions causing inconsistent validation results
risks.rationale: If validation chunks have dependencies, parallel processing may validate data in incorrect order or with stale context

non_risks.description: Validation will not block warning dissemination under normal data volumes
non_risks.rationale: Inline pipeline architecture ensures validation occurs concurrently with transmission, meeting 10-15 second budget

sensitivity_points.parameter: Data chunk size for parallel validation

scenario_reference.text: Unauthorized bulletin generation attempts are blocked 100% of the time

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with external access attempts
analysis_records.stimulus: Unauthorized user attempts to generate or modify bulletin
architectural_decisions: Authentication and Authorization Framework with multi-factor authentication
Role-based access control (RBAC) for bulletin operations
Cryptographic signing of bulletins for integrity verification
analysis_records.reasoning: 100% blocking requires defense-in-depth: authentication verifies identity, authorization verifies permissions, and cryptographic signing ensures bulletins cannot be forged. Multi-factor authentication prevents credential compromise. RBAC ensures only authorized roles can generate bulletins.

risks.description: Insider threats with legitimate credentials could generate unauthorized bulletins
risks.rationale: Authentication framework cannot distinguish between legitimate and malicious use of valid credentials by authorized users

risks.description: Authentication system vulnerabilities could allow credential bypass
risks.rationale: Implementation flaws in authentication logic (e.g., SQL injection, session hijacking) could allow attackers to bypass controls

non_risks.description: External attackers without credentials cannot generate bulletins
non_risks.rationale: Multi-factor authentication and RBAC provide strong barriers against external unauthorized access

conflicting_attributes: Usability (negative: more friction for legitimate users)

scenario_reference.text: System withstands DDoS attacks while maintaining core warning dissemination functions

analysis_records.stimulus: Large volume of malicious traffic targeting system endpoints
analysis_records.response_measure: Core warning functions (P1, P2) continue to meet latency requirements during attack
architectural_decisions: DDoS Mitigation Layer with traffic filtering and rate limiting
Priority queuing for critical warning messages
Separate network paths for critical vs. non-critical traffic
analysis_records.reasoning: DDoS attacks aim to exhaust system resources. Traffic filtering blocks malicious requests at network edge before they consume application resources. Rate limiting prevents resource exhaustion. Priority queuing ensures warning messages bypass attack traffic. Separate paths isolate critical functions from attack surface.

risks.description: Sophisticated application-layer DDoS attacks may bypass network-layer filtering
risks.rationale: Attacks mimicking legitimate traffic patterns may pass through filters and consume application resources

risks.description: DDoS mitigation layer itself could become bottleneck or single point of failure
risks.rationale: If mitigation layer lacks sufficient capacity or redundancy, it may fail under extreme attack volumes

non_risks.description: Network-layer DDoS attacks will not disrupt warning dissemination
non_risks.rationale: Traffic filtering and priority queuing at network edge prevent attack traffic from reaching critical application components

scenario_reference.text: Reduce unofficial alert dissemination from 5% to <1% through authentication mechanisms

analysis_records.environment: Normal operations with potential for unofficial alerts
analysis_records.stimulus: Users attempt to disseminate alerts without proper authorization
analysis_records.response_measure: Unofficial alert rate reduced from 5% to <1%
architectural_decisions: Authentication and Authorization Framework with multi-factor authentication
Role-based access control for alert dissemination
Audit logging of all alert generation activities
analysis_records.reasoning: Reducing unofficial alerts from 5% to <1% requires stronger identity verification and access controls. Multi-factor authentication makes credential sharing/theft harder. RBAC ensures only authorized roles can disseminate alerts. Audit logging enables detection and investigation of unauthorized attempts.

risks.description: User resistance to authentication friction may lead to workarounds that bypass controls
risks.rationale: If authentication is too cumbersome, users may share credentials or find ways to circumvent controls, undermining security

non_risks.description: Authentication mechanisms will significantly reduce unauthorized alert dissemination
non_risks.rationale: Multi-factor authentication and RBAC create substantial barriers to unauthorized access, making 5% to <1% reduction achievable

conflicting_attributes: Usability (negative: slower alert generation for legitimate users)

scenario_reference.text: Automated test coverage detects 90% of errors (improvement from current 25%)

analysis_records.environment: Development and testing phase before deployment
analysis_records.response_measure: Automated tests detect 90% of errors before production deployment
architectural_decisions: Comprehensive automated test suite with unit, integration, and end-to-end tests
Continuous integration pipeline with mandatory test gates
Test coverage monitoring and enforcement
analysis_records.reasoning: Improving error detection from 25% to 90% requires significant expansion of test coverage across all system layers. Unit tests validate component logic, integration tests validate component interactions, end-to-end tests validate complete workflows. CI pipeline enforces test execution before deployment. Coverage monitoring ensures tests remain comprehensive as code evolves.

risks.description: Test suite maintenance burden may become unsustainable as system complexity grows
risks.rationale: Comprehensive test coverage requires large test codebases that must be maintained alongside production code, potentially slowing development

risks.description: Tests may have false confidence if they do not accurately represent production scenarios
risks.rationale: If test environments or data do not match production conditions, tests may pass while production errors remain undetected

non_risks.description: Automated testing will significantly improve error detection before deployment
non_risks.rationale: Expanding from 25% to 90% coverage with comprehensive test types will catch most common error categories

conflicting_attributes: Development velocity (negative: more tests to write and maintain)

scenario_reference.text: System modifications deployable without exceeding 15 minutes of downtime using zero-downtime deployment architecture

analysis_records.stimulus: New system version ready for deployment
architectural_decisions: Blue-green or rolling update deployment strategy
Stateless application design for easy instance replacement
Database migration automation with backward compatibility
analysis_records.reasoning: 15-minute downtime constraint requires deployment strategies that minimize service interruption. Blue-green deploys new version alongside old, switching traffic after validation. Rolling updates gradually replace instances. Stateless design allows instances to be replaced without session loss. Backward-compatible database migrations allow old and new code to coexist during transition.

risks.description: Database schema changes may require longer downtime if backward compatibility is not maintained
risks.rationale: Breaking database changes require all application instances to be updated simultaneously, preventing gradual rollout

risks.description: Deployment rollback may exceed 15-minute window if issues are detected late
risks.rationale: If problems are discovered after deployment completes, rolling back may take longer than forward deployment

non_risks.description: Application code updates will not exceed 15-minute downtime
non_risks.rationale: Blue-green or rolling update strategies enable near-zero downtime for stateless application deployments
